questions.question: Explain the difference between the return values of the fork() system call in the parent and child processes.
expectedPoints: It returns the process ID (PID) of the newly created child process to the parent.
It returns 0 to the child process.
This difference in return value is how code distinguishes between parent and child execution paths.

questions.question: Describe the combined fork-exec mechanism. Why is using fork() alone not sufficient to execute an external command like 'ls'? What is the role of the wait() system call in this context?
expectedPoints: The fork() system call creates a new process, but this process is a copy of the parent and runs the same program.
To run a different program (e.g., 'ls'), the child process must use an exec() function to replace its memory space with the new program's code.
The classic pattern is: parent calls fork(), child calls exec(), parent calls wait().
fork() alone is insufficient because it only duplicates the current program; it does not load a new one.
The wait() system call allows the parent to suspend its execution until the child process terminates.
It ensures synchronization, allows the parent to collect the child's exit status, and prevents the child from becoming a zombie process.
